section.title: The Great Banquet and the Cost of Discipleship
section.sectionRationale: At a Sabbath meal in a Pharisee's house, Jesus heals a man with dropsy, then overturns the table manners of the proud: take the lowest place, and invite those who cannot repay you. When a guest speaks of the blessedness of the kingdom's feast, Jesus tells of a great banquet whose invited guests all make excuses, so the master fills his house with the poor from the streets and the highways. Then, to the crowds who follow so easily, He names the cost: whoever will not carry his cross and give up everything cannot be His disciple.
section.thesis: The kingdom is a banquet of free grace that gathers the poor and the outsider while the self-satisfied excuse themselves, and it lays a total claim on all who would follow, so that grace which costs the sinner nothing costs the disciple everything.
section.frameNote: Four movements: healing on the Sabbath and the places at the table; the invitation of those who cannot repay; the parable of the great banquet; and the cost of discipleship, with the warning of savorless salt.

units.label: The Sabbath meal and the places of honor
units.note: the one who humbles himself will be exalted

verses.text: One Sabbath, Jesus went to eat in the home of a leading Pharisee, and those in attendance were watching Him closely.
Right there before Him was a man with dropsy.
So Jesus asked the experts in the law and the Pharisees, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not?”
But they remained silent. Then Jesus took hold of the man, healed him, and sent him on his way.
And He asked them, “Which of you whose son or ox falls into a pit on the Sabbath day will not immediately pull him out?”
And they were unable to answer these questions.
When Jesus noticed how the guests chose the places of honor, He told them a parable:
“When you are invited to a wedding banquet, do not sit in the place of honor, in case someone more distinguished than you has been invited.
Then the host who invited both of you will come and tell you, ‘Give this man your seat.’ And in humiliation, you will have to take the last place.

But when you are invited, go and sit in the last place, so that your host will come and tell you, ‘Friend, move up to a better place.’ Then you will be honored in front of everyone at the table with you.
For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and the one who {{tapeinoo|humbles}} himself will be exalted.”

units.label: Inviting those who cannot repay
units.note: you will be repaid at the resurrection

verses.text: Then Jesus said to the man who had invited Him, “When you host a dinner or a banquet, do not invite your friends or brothers or relatives or rich neighbors. Otherwise, they may invite you in return, and you will be repaid.
But when you host a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind,
and you will be blessed. Since they cannot repay you, you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.”

units.label: The parable of the great banquet

verses.text: When one of those reclining with Him heard this, he said to Jesus, “Blessed is everyone who will eat at the feast in the kingdom of God.”
But Jesus replied, “A certain man prepared a great banquet and invited many guests.
When it was time for the banquet, he sent his servant to tell those who had been invited, ‘Come, for everything is now ready.’
But one after another they all began to make excuses. The first one said, ‘I have bought a field, and I need to go see it. Please excuse me.’
Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to try them out. Please excuse me.’
Still another said, ‘I have married a wife, so I cannot come.’

The servant returned and reported all this to his master. Then the owner of the house became angry and said to his servant, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the city, and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.’
‘Sir,’ the servant replied, ‘what you ordered has been done, and there is still room.’
So the master told his servant, ‘Go out to the highways and hedges and {{anankazo|compel}} them to come in, so that my house will be full.
For I tell you, not one of those men who were invited will taste my banquet.’”

units.label: The cost of discipleship and savorless salt
units.note: whoever does not carry his cross

verses.text: Now large crowds were traveling with Jesus, and He turned and said to them,
“If anyone comes to Me and does not {{miseo|hate}} his father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, yes, even his own life, he cannot be My disciple.
And whoever does not carry his {{stauros|cross}} and follow Me cannot be My disciple.
Which of you, wishing to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost to see if he has the resources to complete it?
Otherwise, if he lays the foundation and is unable to finish the work, everyone who sees it will ridicule him,
saying, ‘This man could not finish what he started to build.’
Or what king on his way to war with another king will not first sit down and consider whether he can engage with ten thousand men the one coming against him with twenty thousand?
And if he is unable, he will send a delegation while the other king is still far off, to ask for terms of peace.
In the same way, any one of you who does not give up everything he has cannot be My disciple.
Salt is good, but if the salt loses its savor, with what will it be seasoned?
It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile, and it is thrown out. He who has ears to hear, let him hear.”

tapeinoo.gloss: To humble, to lower. Jesus lays down the law of the kingdom against the scramble for status at the table: everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and the one who lowers himself will be lifted up by God. This is the pattern of Christ Himself, who humbled Himself and was exalted.

anankazo.gloss: To compel, to constrain, to urge strongly. The master sends his servant to the highways and hedges to compel the outsiders in, not by force of violence but by the pressing, gracious urgency of the invitation, so that his house will be full. The word underlines that grace goes out and draws the reluctant and the unlikely.

miseo.gloss: To hate. In Semitic idiom to hate is to love less, to subordinate. Jesus is not commanding hostility to family but demanding that loyalty to Him surpass every other bond, even the love of one's own life. Christ must be first, or He is not truly followed at all.

stauros.gloss: Cross. To carry one's cross is to walk as a condemned man walks to execution, dead to the old self and its claims. Jesus makes cross-bearing the mark of a true disciple, a daily death that anticipates His own and shares in it.

basic.context: At a Pharisee's Sabbath table, Jesus heals a man with dropsy and silences those who watched to trap Him. Seeing guests grab the best seats, He teaches them to take the lowest place, for God exalts the humble. He tells the host to invite those who cannot repay, the poor and the lame, promising repayment at the resurrection. When a guest sighs about the blessedness of the kingdom's feast, Jesus tells of a great banquet: the invited guests all make excuses, so the master fills his house with the poor from the streets and strangers from the highways. Then, to the crowds who follow so lightly, He names the cost: whoever will not put Him above family and self, carry his cross, and give up everything cannot be His disciple. Salt that loses its savor is thrown out.
basic.hermeneutics: Hold the two halves together. The banquet parables proclaim grace: the feast is free, and it fills up with those who could never earn a place. The discipleship sayings name the cost: this free grace lays total claim on the one who receives it. Grace that costs the sinner nothing costs the disciple everything.
basic.originalLanguages: The law of the table is that the one who tapeinoo, humbles himself, will be exalted. The master sends out to anankazo, to compel, the outsiders in by gracious urgency. To follow Jesus one must miseo, love less, every rival loyalty, even life itself, and carry the stauros, the cross, the daily death of a condemned man.
basic.typology: The kingdom feast fulfills the prophets' picture of a great banquet God spreads for all peoples on His mountain. The rejected invitation and the gathering of outsiders foreshadow the gospel going out from Israel to the nations. Cross-bearing points to Christ's own cross, the pattern every disciple takes up.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Lord of the Sabbath who heals and the host of the kingdom banquet who throws His house open to the poor. He is the One who humbled Himself and was exalted, the pattern of the table's law. And He is the King worth more than father, mother, and life itself, who calls His followers to the cross He will Himself carry.
basic.god: God exalts the humble and welcomes those who cannot repay, delighting to fill His house with the overlooked. He is generous beyond calculation, promising repayment at the resurrection. And He is worthy of total allegiance, a Father whose feast is free yet whose claim is absolute.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 25 pictures the LORD's feast of rich food for all peoples. Philippians 2 shows Christ humbled and then exalted. Luke 9 gives the parallel call to take up the cross daily. James 4 promises that God lifts up the humble.
basic.oneStory: On the road to Jerusalem Jesus spreads the kingdom's table wide, gathering the poor and the outsider while the comfortable excuse themselves. The same grace that fills His house for free lays a cross on every guest, so that the story runs from the open invitation of the banquet to the total surrender of the disciple who follows the humbled and exalted King.

context.literary: The whole chapter unfolds at and around a meal, a favorite Lukan setting for teaching. It moves from a Sabbath healing to table etiquette, to the guest list of grace, to the parable of the banquet, and finally out to the crowds on the road for the cost of discipleship. The banquet motif ties the parts together: who gets in, and what it costs.
context.historical: Meals in this world were carefully ranked by honor, and reclining places broadcast status. Inviting only those who could reciprocate was the ordinary economy of patronage. Dropsy, a swelling from fluid, made the man ceremonially and socially marginal. The highways and hedges evoke the poor and the wanderer beyond the city, and beyond that, the Gentiles.
context.canonical: The messianic banquet is a deep biblical hope, from Isaiah's feast on the mountain to the marriage supper of the Lamb in Revelation. The reversal of the exalted and the humble runs through Scripture, from Hannah's song to the Magnificat. Cross-bearing gathers up the Gospels' repeated call to lose one's life to find it.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To unveil the kingdom as a banquet of free grace that welcomes the poor and the outsider while the self-satisfied exclude themselves, and to press on all who would follow the total, cross-shaped cost of true discipleship.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The healing and the banquet are narrated to reveal the character of God's kingdom, but Jesus draws direct commands from them: humble yourself, invite those who cannot repay, come to the feast, count the cost. The parable describes grace; the discipleship sayings prescribe the surrender it demands.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean by commanding disciples to 'hate' father and mother?

views.label: A Semitic idiom for loving less, radical priority
views.case: In Hebrew and Aramaic thought, to hate can mean to love less or to subordinate, as when Jacob is said to love Leah less. Matthew's parallel reads 'loves father or mother more than Me', confirming that Jesus demands supreme allegiance, not literal hostility. He elsewhere upholds honoring parents.

views.label: A deliberately shocking absolute
views.case: The starkness is intended: discipleship may set a follower against his own household, and Jesus refuses to soften the break that loyalty to Him can require. The word is chosen to jolt casual crowds into weighing the true cost.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: Christ must hold first place over every other love, including the love of one's own life, or discipleship is not real. This is priority, not permission for cruelty.

debates.question: What does 'compel them to come in' authorize?

views.label: The urgent, gracious persuasion of the gospel
views.case: The master's aim is a full house of the unlikely; the compelling is the pressing insistence of an invitation that overcomes the poor's reluctance to believe they are truly welcome. The context is grace overflowing to those who assume they do not belong.

views.label: A text tragically misused to justify coercion
views.case: In history this line was wrongly wielded to force conversions. Read rightly against the whole passage of free invitation and undeserving guests, it cannot mean violence; it means the earnest, winning urgency of a servant sent to gather the reluctant.

debates.bearing: The verse fuels bold, warm evangelism to those who feel unqualified, never coercion; the God of this banquet draws the outsider by grace, not by force.

study.originalLanguages: The table's governing law is that the one who tapeinoo, humbles or lowers himself, will be exalted by God, while the self-exalting are brought low, the very pattern of Christ. In the parable the master sends his servant to anankazo, to compel, the outsiders in, a word of pressing, gracious urgency rather than violence, so that his house will be full. To follow Jesus one must miseo his nearest kin, an idiom for loving them less, subordinating every bond to Christ, even the love of one's own life. And the disciple must carry the stauros, the cross, walking as a condemned man walks, dead to the old self and its claims. Free grace and total cost meet in these words.

typology.type: The LORD's feast of rich food for all peoples, Isaiah 25:6
typology.fulfillment: The great banquet of the kingdom thrown open to the poor and the outsider, Luke 14:15-24.

typology.type: Christ who humbled Himself and was exalted, Philippians 2:8-9
typology.fulfillment: The law of the table, everyone who humbles himself will be exalted, Luke 14:11.

typology.type: The cross Jesus carries to Golgotha
typology.fulfillment: The disciple's daily cross, the mark of true following, Luke 14:27.

study.christ: Jesus is the Lord of the Sabbath who heals the swollen and marginal man while His critics watch, and He is the host of the kingdom's banquet, flinging His doors open to the poor, the lame, and the stranger from the highways. He embodies the law He teaches, for He is the One who humbled Himself to the lowest place and was exalted to the highest. And He is the King whose worth exceeds father, mother, spouse, and life itself, who calls disciples to carry a cross because He is about to carry His own. To sit at His feast is free; to follow Him is to die daily and rise with Him.
study.god: The God of this passage exalts the humble and pulls the self-important down, welcomes those who can never repay, and promises to settle every account at the resurrection of the righteous. He is a Father whose house must be full, who sends His servants into the streets and the highways to gather the unlikely by grace. His feast costs the guest nothing, and yet He is worthy of everything, an absolute allegiance gladly given because He first spread the table.

crossReferences.note: The LORD spreads a feast of rich food for all peoples and swallows up death, the hope behind the kingdom banquet.
Christ humbled Himself to death on a cross, therefore God exalted Him, the pattern of Luke 14:11.
The parallel call to deny oneself and take up the cross daily and follow Jesus.
Humble yourselves before the Lord, and He will exalt you, the promise of the table's law.

study.oneStory: As Jesus journeys toward Jerusalem He throws the kingdom's table open, gathering the poor and the outsider while the comfortable make their excuses, and He warns that this free grace lays a cross on all who take a seat. The story runs from the wide, undeserved welcome of the banquet to the total surrender of the disciple, both held together in the King who humbled Himself, carried His cross, and was exalted, and who bids His people follow Him the same way.

quiz.q: What lesson does Jesus draw from how the guests chose their seats?

options.text: Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and the one who humbles himself will be exalted.
options.note: Luke 14:11 states the kingdom's reversal directly.

options.text: It is wiser to take a low seat so the host can move you up.
options.note: True to the parable's picture, but it is the servant illustration, not the deeper principle of God humbling and exalting.

options.text: Good manners will win you the respect of others.
options.note: The point is God's exalting of the humble, not social technique to gain esteem.

options.text: Those who seize the best seats deserve them.
options.note: The opposite: the self-exalting will be humbled.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus tell the host to invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind?

options.text: Because they cannot repay, so the host will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.
options.note: Luke 14:13-14 grounds the invitation in reward from God, not reciprocity.

options.text: Because generosity to the needy is blessed by God.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific ground: they cannot repay, and God repays at the resurrection.

options.text: Because the poor make more grateful guests.
options.note: The reason given is the inability to repay and God's future reward, not the guests' gratitude.

options.text: Because inviting rich neighbors is forbidden.
options.note: Jesus warns against inviting only those who can repay, not against rich people as such.

quiz.q: What happens in the parable of the great banquet?

options.text: The invited guests make excuses, so the master fills his house with the poor and the outsiders.
options.note: Luke 14:18-23 shows the refusal of the first-invited and the gathering of the unlikely.

options.text: The master gathers the poor from the streets into his house.
options.note: True, but it leaves out the crucial refusal of the originally invited guests.

options.text: The guests were too busy but promised to come later.
options.note: They did not defer; they refused, and none of them will taste the banquet.

options.text: The banquet was canceled because no one came.
options.note: The house is filled to overflowing; the feast goes ahead with new guests.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say it costs to be His disciple?

options.text: To put Him above family and self, carry one's cross, and give up everything.
options.note: Luke 14:26-33 names supreme allegiance, cross-bearing, and total surrender.

options.text: To love Christ more than father, mother, and one's own life.
options.note: True and central, but it omits cross-bearing and the surrender of everything.

options.text: To perform acts of self-denial from time to time.
options.note: Jesus asks not occasional gestures but a daily cross and the giving up of all.

options.text: Nothing; following Jesus makes no real demands.
options.note: The whole passage is about counting the cost, which is total.

quiz.q: What is the warning of the savorless salt?

options.text: A disciple who loses what makes him distinct becomes useless and is thrown out.
options.note: Luke 14:34-35 warns against a discipleship that has lost its savor.

options.text: Salt is a good and useful thing.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but the warning is about salt that has lost its savor.

options.text: Disciples should season their speech with grace.
options.note: A worthy idea elsewhere, but here the image warns of uselessness, not of gracious speech.

options.text: Salt can never lose its usefulness.
options.note: The point is precisely that savorless salt is fit for nothing and discarded.

questions: Jesus watched people reach for the best seats. Where are you quietly maneuvering for status, and what would it look like to take the lowest place and trust God to lift you?
The host is told to invite those who cannot repay. Who might God be asking you to welcome with no return in view, looking instead to the resurrection?
In the parable the guests were not wicked, just busy with a field, oxen, and a marriage. Which good and ordinary things are you letting crowd out God's invitation?
Jesus tells the eager crowds to count the cost. Have you ever honestly reckoned what following Him may cost you, and does He hold first place over every other love?
Grace fills the banquet for free, yet it lays a cross on every guest. Where is Christ calling you to a daily death this week, and what makes that hard to carry?

translationNotes.note: A footnote records that some manuscripts read 'donkey' rather than 'son'; either way the point is that people rescue what is theirs on the Sabbath, so surely a suffering person may be healed.

The Greek verb rendered 'hate' is a Semitic idiom for loving less or subordinating. Matthew's parallel, 'loves father or mother more than Me', confirms the sense: Christ demands supreme allegiance, not literal hatred, and elsewhere He upholds honoring parents. The em dashes of the printed text have been rendered as commas here.
'He who has ears to hear, let him hear' is Jesus' recurring call to attend and obey, sharpening the warning that a savorless discipleship is discarded.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 14:1-35. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dashes in verse 26 were converted to commas. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
